message:
chore: sync audit index (batch 393) and beads issue tracker

audit_index.jsonl: 6 new entries from batch 393 diff audit
- compiler.rs (1000L SOUND, 1 LOW cosmetic)
- explain.rs (443L SOUND)
- control.rs (1800L SOUND, delta-CRDT layer)
- federation.rs (1250L SOUND, test fix)
- replay.rs (2200L SOUND, removed unnecessary clone)
- timeout_future.rs (750L FIXED, 1 bug: panic→fail-closed)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"asupersync-1qfd0.395","title":"[audit][link] Self-link establishment must not duplicate self-exit actions","description":"Focused audit follow-up for src/link.rs only. LinkSet::establish_with_policy() currently allows task_a == task_b and indexes the same LinkRef twice in task_index. That makes a single logical self-link appear twice in peers_of()/peers_with_policy() and causes resolve_exits() to emit duplicate self-exit actions for one abnormal termination. Scope: make self-links fail closed or otherwise canonicalize them so one logical self-link cannot duplicate peer/exit behavior, add focused regressions, and keep the write surface to src/link.rs.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"RoseIsland","created_at":"2026-03-17T01:08:56.848727377Z","created_by":"RoseIsland","updated_at":"2026-03-17T01:14:19.447183099Z","closed_at":"2026-03-17T01:14:19.447153464Z","close_reason":"Patched src/link.rs so self-links are indexed once in task_index, eliminating duplicate peers_with_policy/resolve_exits behavior.  {"id":"asupersync-1qfd0.397","title":"[audit][reactor] Registration::deregister must preserve Drop cleanup after persistent deregister failure","description":"Fresh diff audit of src/runtime/reactor/registration.rs found that Registration::deregister(self) consumed the handle but still disarmed Drop even when both explicit deregister attempts failed or the reactor panicked. That leaves callers with no retry surface and can strand reactor bookkeeping instead of preserving one final best-effort cleanup pass. Patch should keep Drop armed on persistent explicit deregister failure, add focused regression coverage, and validate with rch-offloaded cargo commands.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"FuchsiaStone","created_at":"2026-03-17T02:16:02.736764796Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-17T02:31:08.359563488Z","closed_at":"2026-03-17T02:31:08.359534123Z","close_reason":"Completed: kept Registration Drop armed on persistent explicit deregister failure/panic, added focused regressions, validated via rch","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["audit","reactor"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"asupersync-1qfd0.397","depends_on_id":"asupersync-1qfd0","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-17T02:16:02.736764796Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":2524,"issue_id":"asupersync-1qfd0.397","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Patched src/runtime/reactor/registration.rs so Registration::deregister(self) only disarms Drop on successful explicit cleanup. Previously the consumed handle still disarmed Drop after persistent deregister error or panic, which removed the last best-effort cleanup path and could strand reactor bookkeeping.

Validation:
- git diff --check passed
- rch exec -- env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/rch-fuchsiastone-registration cargo check -p asupersync --lib passed
- rch exec -- env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/rch-fuchsiastone-registration cargo test -p asupersync --lib explicit_deregister_ -- --nocapture passed with 5 tests
- rch exec -- rustfmt --check src/runtime/reactor/registration.rs passed after fixing import ordering
- focused cargo clippy on the lib is currently blocked by unrelated concurrent DNS work in src/net/dns/resolver.rs:259 (clippy needless pass by value), not by this patch
- repo-wide cargo fmt --check is currently red because concurrent edits in src/net/dns/cache.rs and src/net/dns/resolver.rs are not yet rustfmt-clean

Regression coverage:
- strengthened persistent-error test to prove Drop stays armed and performs the final cleanup pass
- strengthened panic-path test to prove the post-error Drop retry occurs
- added explicit_deregister_error_still_allows_drop_cleanup_success, where the first two explicit deregister attempts fail and the final Drop cleanup attempt succeeds","created_at":"2026-03-17T02:30:33Z"}]}
-{"id":"asupersync-1qfd0.398","title":"[audit][dns] Canonicalize trailing-dot FQDNs in resolver/cache","description":"Root cause:
- resolver host validation rejects standards-valid absolute hostnames when the only extra byte is the trailing root dot
- cache key normalization lowercases hosts but does not canonicalize a trailing dot, so example.com and example.com. fragment cache/negative-cache behavior

Planned fix:
- canonicalize DNS cache/resolver host keys so trailing-dot FQDNs share cache entries with the non-dotted form
- accept max-length absolute hostnames by validating the canonical hostname length instead of the raw dotted input
- add focused regressions for cache aliasing and absolute-hostname length handling","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"TurquoiseHawk","created_at":"2026-03-18T14:59:04.121334149Z","created_by":"RainyRiver","updated_at":"2026-03-18T23:23:39.123970759Z","closed_at":"2026-03-18T23:23:39.123946414Z","close_reason":"Completed: current HEAD contains the DNS absolute-name cache fix plus regressions; actual bug was trailing-dot cache aliasing in resolver lookup/store, while strict clippy/fmt failures remain outside this slice.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["audit","dns"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"asupersync-1qfd0.398","depends_on_id":"asupersync-1qfd0","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-18T14:59:04.121334149Z","created_by":"RainyRiver","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":2544,"issue_id":"asupersync-1qfd0.398","author":"RainyRiver","text":"Start note:
- `bv --robot-triage` and `bv --robot-next` both timed out again in this repo, so I used the ready set plus live code inspection to keep momentum.
- Concrete bug is in DNS canonicalization, not another stale already-landed bead.

Root cause under fix:
- trailing-dot FQDNs fragment resolver/cache behavior because cache key normalization only lowercases
- resolver length validation checks the raw host string before canonicalizing away the trailing root dot","created_at":"2026-03-18T14:59:28Z"},{"id":2545,"issue_id":"asupersync-1qfd0.398","author":"RainyRiver","text":"Scope correction after deeper DNS semantics review:
- I am **not** canonicalizing trailing-dot and non-dotted names into one cache key; that would risk changing search-domain semantics.
- The concrete live bug is narrower and real: `Resolver::do_lookup_ip()` rejected a standards-valid max-length absolute hostname because it counted the trailing root dot before validation.
- Fix in flight keeps query semantics intact and only validates length against the hostname with one trailing root dot trimmed.","created_at":"2026-03-18T15:01:58Z"},{"id":2546,"issue_id":"asupersync-1qfd0.398","author":"RainyRiver","text":"Progress update:
- landed the resolver-side fix in `src/net/dns/resolver.rs`: max-length absolute hostnames now validate after trimming one trailing root dot for length-check purposes only
- added focused regressions for the standards-valid 254-byte absolute hostname and the still-invalid overlong case

Validation status:
- `rustfmt --edition 2024 --check src/net/dns/resolver.rs` passed
- `ubs src/net/dns/resolver.rs` exited 0
- `rch exec -- ... cargo test --lib absolute_hostname -- --nocapture` and `rch exec -- ... cargo check --all-targets` are currently blocked by infrastructure, not code: the scheduler repeatedly routes to worker `vmi1227854`, and `rch fleet status` reports that worker at `0MB` free on `/tmp`, producing `cc` temp-file `ENOSPC` failures before compilation finishes

Reservation narrowed:
- released `src/net/dns/cache.rs`; keeping `src/net/dns/resolver.rs` reserved while the offloaded cargo gates are blocked","created_at":"2026-03-18T15:06:58Z"},{"id":2552,"issue_id":"asupersync-1qfd0.398","author":"TurquoiseHawk","text":"Fresh-eyes DNS audit update:
- confirmed the live bug is the opposite of the bead title: `Resolver::lookup_ip()` was stripping one trailing root dot before cache lookup/store, which aliased `name.` onto `name` and could leak search-domain-sensitive cache results across the absolute/non-absolute boundary
- kept trailing-dot trimming only for hostname-length validation in `do_lookup_ip()`; cache identity now stays on the original query host
- added focused regressions in `src/net/dns/resolver.rs` proving dotted queries do not reuse non-dotted positive or negative cache entries, plus a `src/net/dns/cache.rs` regression proving case-insensitive lookups still preserve dotted-vs-non-dotted key separation

Validation so far:
- `git diff --check -- src/net/dns/resolver.rs src/net/dns/cache.rs` PASS
- `ubs --only=rust src/net/dns` ran clean of new critical findings but is noisy on existing test unwrap/assert inventory and one unrelated lifecycle heuristic in resolver connect code
- `rch exec -- cargo check --all-targets` reached remote `exit=0`; local tail then reported the usual artifact-retrieval vanished-file warning
- `rch exec -- cargo fmt --check` is red outside this slice in `src/http/h1/http_client.rs`, `src/net/websocket/*`, and `tests/websocket_acceptor_trailing_bytes.rs`
- `rch exec -- cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` is red outside this slice in `src/stream/debounce.rs` and `src/net/websocket/handshake.rs`
- focused `rch` test lanes for the new DNS regressions are still running unusually slowly / quietly after compile on the remote worker; no DNS-specific failure has surfaced yet","created_at":"2026-03-18T23:20:33Z"}]}

 {"id":"asupersync-1qfd0.4","title":"[audit][service] Reserve buffer capacity in poll_ready so clone races cannot revoke readiness","description":"Buffer::poll_ready currently reports Ready based only on the shared pending count, but it does not actually reserve a slot. Two clones can both observe Ready against capacity=1, after which one caller can fill the buffer and make the other caller's immediately-following call() fail with BufferError::Full. That violates the usual Service contract expectation that a successful poll_ready authorizes the next call on that handle. Fix by reserving a slot during poll_ready, consuming it in call(), releasing it on drop/close, and adding clone-race regression tests.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"CloudyPond","created_at":"2026-03-08T01:50:03.548796130Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-08T02:08:16.874795269Z","closed_at":"2026-03-08T02:08:16.874685616Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["audit","buffer","service"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"asupersync-1qfd0.4","depends_on_id":"asupersync-1qfd0","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-08T01:50:03.548796130Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"asupersync-1qfd0.40","title":"[audit][trace] Keep TraceRecorder::take metadata rotation deterministic","description":"TraceRecorder::take() currently resets ReplayTrace metadata for the fresh recorder by overwriting recorded_at with SystemTime::now(). That reintroduces ambient wall-clock authority even when the caller supplied deterministic TraceMetadata, so repeated take()/rotate cycles produce nondeterministic trace headers. Patch src/trace/recorder.rs so recorder rotation preserves explicit metadata deterministically instead of consulting wall clock, add a focused regression around repeated take() behavior, and validate with rch-backed targeted check/clippy/test commands.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"PinkForest","estimated_minutes":45,"created_at":"2026-03-09T02:56:30.373627443Z","created_by":"PinkForest","updated_at":"2026-03-09T03:02:12.568558944Z","closed_at":"2026-03-09T03:02:12.568533607Z","close_reason":"TraceRecorder::take no longer reintroduces ambient wall clock; deterministic rotation regression passed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"asupersync-1qfd0.40","depends_on_id":"asupersync-1qfd0","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T02:56:30.373627443Z","created_by":"PinkForest","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"asupersync-1qfd0.41","title":"[audit][trace] Make ReplayCheckpoint creation deterministic for identical trace positions","description":"ReplayCheckpoint::new currently stamps created_at from SystemTime::now(), so taking the same checkpoint twice against the same trace position yields different serialized checkpoint bytes and leaks ambient wall clock back into the deterministic replay toolchain. Patch src/trace/streaming.rs so checkpoint creation is deterministic for a given trace metadata + events_processed pair, add focused regression coverage for identical-position stability and monotonic differentiation across positions, and validate with rch-backed targeted check/clippy/test commands.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"PinkForest","estimated_minutes":45,"created_at":"2026-03-09T03:06:17.083408797Z","created_by":"PinkForest","updated_at":"2026-03-09T03:13:10.603716194Z","closed_at":"2026-03-09T03:13:10.603691518Z","close_reason":"ReplayCheckpoint no longer uses ambient wall clock; identical-position checkpoint serialization is deterministic and targeted replay checkpoint tests passed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["audit","determinism","streaming","trace"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"asupersync-1qfd0.41","depends_on_id":"asupersync-1qfd0","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T03:06:17.083408797Z","created_by":"PinkForest","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"asupersync-2a6k9.4.5","title":"Build live evidence capture and normalization unit suite with golden records","description":"Test the live-capture and normalization pipeline directly with deterministic fixtures, negative cases, and field-level mismatch diagnostics.","design":"Add the deterministic verification suite for live-side capture and normalization. This should cover positive and negative cases for evidence extraction, missing-observability behavior, canonical ordering, lossy normalization decisions, and representative golden records that prove lab and live evidence collapse into the intended shared shape.

The suite should also require detailed diff logging when normalization mismatches occur. If a normalization regression happens, the output should show exactly which semantic field diverged and which source evidence produced it.","acceptance_criteria":"Live evidence capture and normalization have dedicated unit/fixture coverage rather than being tested only transitively through later pilots.

Representative golden lab/live records are checked into the verification story and are easy to diff when behavior changes.

Normalization failures emit detailed field-level diagnostics that make root-cause analysis fast, and at least one targeted end-to-end path proves the same capture/normalization surface behaves correctly when exercised through the real runner flow.","notes":"Normalization is too central to leave under-tested.  {"id":"asupersync-2a6k9.5","title":"Implement differential execution, divergence classification, and repro artifacts","description":"Assemble the execution engine that runs the same scenario through lab and live paths, classifies mismatches, emits minimized repro bundles when parity fails, and exposes reusable end-to-end runner/report entrypoints that make the system practical for humans and CI.","design":"This phase assembles the actual differential judge. Its job is to run both paths, compare normalized records, and preserve evidence aggressively when parity fails.

The design should treat repro quality as part of correctness. A differential test that only says "mismatch" without a seed, witness, or replay bundle creates more frustration than confidence. The system should therefore integrate minimization, classification, and artifact generation tightly rather than bolt them on after the fact.

This phase should also guard against false positives by distinguishing unsupported surfaces, missing observability, and scheduler-only noise from true semantic divergence.","acceptance_criteria":"A reusable differential runner exists and can execute the same scenario in lab and live paths.

 {"id":"asupersync-8w83i.15","title":"[FABRIC][P14] CRDT-Augmented Mergeable Control Plane","description":"# Phase 14: CRDT-Augmented Mergeable Control Plane

## Purpose
Add delta-CRDT convergent state for non-authoritative metadata surfaces. The proposal gets materially stronger by saying exactly where strong sequencing is required and where convergent merge is enough.

## Authoritative State (remains in fenced leadered control capsules)
- Appends, authority transfer, reply rights, cursor authority, epoch changes

## Non-Authoritative Surfaces (converge as delta-CRDTs)
- Interest summaries
- Coarse cursor checkpoints
- Membership views
- Lag and load sketches
- Selected advisory aggregates
- Delegated-partition aggregates

## Design Sweet Spot
- Strong consistency where ambiguity would be dangerous
- Convergent merge where centralization would become bottleneck
- Concrete answer to hardest practical question: preventing control plane from becoming global metadata bottleneck

## Scaling Story
- Optimistic propagation across stewards and relays
- Leaderless convergence on cold paths and during transient partitions
- Dramatically cheaper high-fanout consumer churn
- Much lower pressure on authoritative sequencer lease

## Guardrails
- CRDTs ONLY on explicitly non-authoritative, mergeable surfaces
- Never absorb authoritative state into CRDT
- CRDTs are complement to RaptorQ data capsules and fenced control capsules

## Relation to Existing Code
- obligation/crdt.rs already has CRDT concepts
- distributed/ module has convergent state patterns

## FABRIC North Star Reference
This phase is governed by the five cross-cutting FABRIC success criteria defined in `src/messaging/mod.rs` and `docs/FABRIC_GUARDRAILS.md`:
1. Keep the public mental model NATS-small.
2. Make stronger guarantees explicit named service classes, never hidden taxes on the common case.
3. Lower radical behavior into small, inspectable artifacts.
4. Keep autonomous policy loops inside declared safety envelopes with replay, evidence, and rollback.

 {"id":"asupersync-8w83i.16","title":"[FABRIC][P15] Advanced Semantic Features","description":"# Phase 15: Advanced Semantic Features

## Purpose
Build the advanced features that make the fabric a new category of distributed systems substrate: evidence-native data plane, cut-certified mobility, workflow/saga kernel, counterfactual control, distributed supervision, bounded-regret reliability, consistency certificates, recoverable service capsules, intent-compiled operations, etc.

## Features
1. Evidence-Native Data Plane: FrankenSuite evidence for routing/delivery decisions
2. Cut-Certified Mobility: hot-cell evacuation with proof no obligations orphaned
3. Subject-Native Workflow/Saga Kernel: durable workflow steps as subject transitions + obligations
4. Continuous Counterfactual Control: shadow branches from certified cuts, compare candidate worlds
5. Distributed Supervision Compiler: supervision graphs + subject morphisms + failure domains
6. Bounded-Regret Reliability Control: adaptive tuning within declared safety envelopes
7. Local-to-Global Consistency Certificates: sheaf/obstruction-based topology checking
8. Recoverable Service Capsules: portable hosted state with authority-scrubbing on restore
9. Intent-Compiled Operations: operator intent surface compiled to policy artifacts
10. Commutativity-Aware Sharding: semantic independence partitioning above canonical cell ownership
11. Branch-Addressable Reality: live/lagged/replayed/canary/forensic branch attachment
12. Causality-Native Streams: partial-order views above linear control kernels
13. Budget-Carrying Traffic: overload decisions driven by semantic damage not just load
14. Certified Cut Lattice & Reality Index: policy-scoped retained index of meaningful cuts
15. Privacy-Budgeted Metadata Surfaces: differential-privacy noise on exported metadata
16. Quantitative Obligation Contracts: SLO-style probabilistic contracts above binary obligations
17. Reactive Protocol Synthesis: generate handler skeletons from protocol contracts
18. Speculative Subject-Cell Execution: tentative publish with deterministic rollback

Each of these is a separate task within this epic.

## FABRIC North Star Reference
This phase is governed by the five cross-cutting FABRIC success criteria defined in `src/messaging/mod.rs` and `docs/FABRIC_GUARDRAILS.md`:
1. Keep the public mental model NATS-small.
2. Make stronger guarantees explicit named service classes, never hidden taxes on the common case.
3. Lower radical behavior into small, inspectable artifacts.
4. Keep autonomous policy loops inside declared safety envelopes with replay, evidence, and rollback.
5. Target the first product wedge at systems that need sovereignty, durable partial progress, and post-incident explanation together.

If work in this phase violates those criteria, or drifts toward the anti-goal of a grand unified fabric that makes the common case slower and less legible than NATS, it must be redesigned.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-17T07:03:30.064771820Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-18T06:18:58.317505983Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["advanced","fabric","semantics"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"asupersync-8w83i.16","depends_on_id":"asupersync-8w83i","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-17T07:03:30.064771820Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"asupersync-8w83i.16","depends_on_id":"asupersync-8w83i.10","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-17T07:16:06.828016150Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"asupersync-8w83i.16","depends_on_id":"asupersync-8w83i.13","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-17T07:15:31.495403568Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"asupersync-8w83i.7","title":"[FABRIC][P6] Consumers as Policy-Driven Delivery Engines","description":"# Phase 6: Consumers as Policy-Driven Delivery Engines

## Purpose
Build fabric-hosted consumers as region-owned delivery engines backed by obligations, not just counters. NATS consumer policy is richer than most realize — preserve all of that richness and make it even stronger with decision contracts.

## Core Design
- Consumers are region-owned delivery engines
- Ack state backed by obligations, not only counters
- Delivery attempts carry explicit attempt identity and stale-ack idempotence rules
- Pull requests, flow control, pause windows, heartbeats, and scheduling policies are explicit policy objects
- Delivery selection incorporates budgets, backlog, ack debt, and fairness policy

## Decision-Contract-Driven Scheduling
The deepest NATS-inspired move: treat consumers as scheduling entities
- Pull-consumer priority groups → typed demand classes
- Pull-consumer pinned-client policy → temporary capability leasing
- Pull-consumer overflow policy → bounded fallback rule with evidence
- Redelivery policy → decision contract recording why retry/delay/dead-letter was chosen

## Adaptive Consumer Kernel
- Stable deterministic default policies
- Optional advanced policies that remain audit-backed and replayable
- No hand-wavy exactly-once claims
- Very strong reasoning about pending work and retry causality

## Consumer Modes
- Push mode: messages pushed to subscriber with backpressure
- Pull mode: consumer pulls specific sequence windows or demand classes
- Priority groups within pull consumers
- Pinned-client delivery
- Overflow and prioritized policies

## Relation to Existing Code
- combinator/circuit_breaker.rs has similar adaptive policy patterns
- service/retry.rs has retry policy concepts
- obligation/ledger.rs tracks ack obligations
- franken_decision has decision contract infrastructure

## FABRIC North Star Reference
This phase is governed by the five cross-cutting FABRIC success criteria defined in `src/messaging/mod.rs` and `docs/FABRIC_GUARDRAILS.md`:
1. Keep the public mental model NATS-small.
2. Make stronger guarantees explicit named service classes, never hidden taxes on the common case.
3. Lower radical behavior into small, inspectable artifacts.
4. Keep autonomous policy loops inside declared safety envelopes with replay, evidence, and rollback.
5. Target the first product wedge at systems that need sovereignty, durable partial progress, and post-incident explanation together.

 {"id":"asupersync-8w83i.8","title":"[FABRIC][P7] Control Plane as First-Class Subjects","description":"# Phase 7: Control Plane as First-Class Subjects

## Purpose
Model the control plane as first-class subjects in the same fabric, not a separate imperative API. NATS's $SYS.* pattern is powerful — extend it with region-owned handlers, FrankenSuite evidence, and capability domains.

## Control Subject Families
- Fabric health
- Import/export changes
- Route and gateway advisories
- Lameduck and drain state
- Auth and capability failures
- Consumer pause and unpin events
- Stream snapshot creation and restore
- Repair, replay, and rebalance advisories

## Design Guardrails
- System/control subjects live in dedicated capability domain
- Control handlers run with reserved budget and priority (not competing with user traffic)
- Minimal bootstrap and break-glass recovery path available even when ordinary fabric is degraded
- Advisory subjects do NOT automatically feed policy loops without explicit damping/stratification/operator intent

## Asupersync-Unique Additions
1. Control-plane handlers and advisories are region-owned and replayable (once external inputs modeled)
2. Material decisions emit FrankenSuite evidence and decision records

Operators see more than "gateway detached" — they see:
- Which capability graph edges were affected
- Which obligations were transferred, aborted, or replay-scheduled
- Which policy decided the failover path
- What evidence justified it

## Relation to Existing Code
- grpc/health.rs has health check infrastructure
- server/shutdown.rs has graceful shutdown with mark_stopped wakeup
- signal/graceful.rs has graceful signal handling
- franken_evidence and franken_decision provide evidence/decision machinery

## FABRIC North Star Reference
This phase is governed by the five cross-cutting FABRIC success criteria defined in `src/messaging/mod.rs` and `docs/FABRIC_GUARDRAILS.md`:
1. Keep the public mental model NATS-small.
2. Make stronger guarantees explicit named service classes, never hidden taxes on the common case.
3. Lower radical behavior into small, inspectable artifacts.
4. Keep autonomous policy loops inside declared safety envelopes with replay, evidence, and rollback.
5. Target the first product wedge at systems that need sovereignty, durable partial progress, and post-incident explanation together.

If work in this phase violates those criteria, or drifts toward the anti-goal of a grand unified fabric that makes the common case slower and less legible than NATS, it must be redesigned.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-17T06:58:44.838338372Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-18T06:18:33.063968201Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["advisories","control-plane","fabric"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"asupersync-8w83i.8","depends_on_id":"asupersync-8w83i","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-17T06:58:44.838338372Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"asupersync-8w83i.8","depends_on_id":"asupersync-8w83i.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-17T07:06:43.996557063Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"asupersync-8w83i.8","depends_on_id":"asupersync-8w83i.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-17T07:07:19.517838253Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
